seo.title: How to Add Guardrails to Agno Agents
seo.description: Learn how to add runtime guardrails to Agno agents with Vex. Detect hallucinations, prevent drift, and auto-correct bad outputs.

content.intro: Agno's lightweight, model-agnostic architecture makes it easy to spin up multi-modal agents that can process images, audio, and structured data — but that flexibility also means failures can surface in unexpected ways across modalities and team coordination layers. Without runtime guardrails, fabricated memory recalls, malformed nested outputs, and contradictory sub-agent results can silently corrupt production workflows. Vex integrates directly with Agno's agent.run() lifecycle to catch these failures before they reach your users.

points.title: Multi-Modal Input Ambiguity
points.explanation: Agno agents built with Image() or Audio() message types can misinterpret visual or audio content, producing confident but factually wrong outputs. Guardrails let you validate that the agent's interpretation aligns with expected semantic constraints before the response is returned.

points.title: Session Memory Hallucination
points.explanation: Agno's AgentMemory and session storage (SqliteStorage, PostgresStorage) can be queried by the agent to recall past interactions. Without verification, agents may fabricate plausible-sounding but entirely invented memories, especially when session data is sparse or ambiguous.

points.title: Structured Output Schema Failures
points.explanation: Agno supports Pydantic response_model definitions for structured outputs. Complex nested schemas with optional fields, discriminated unions, or recursive models frequently fail silent validation, returning partially populated objects that downstream code treats as complete.

points.title: Team Agent Coordination Drift
points.explanation: Agno's Team class orchestrates multiple specialized agents whose sub-results are synthesized into a final response. Without guardrails, contradictory conclusions from sub-agents can be merged into incoherent final outputs with no error surfaced to the caller.

failures.name: Multi-Modal Misinterpretation
failures.description: When an Agno agent receives an Image() or Audio() input, the underlying model may hallucinate content not present in the media, such as reading text that doesn't exist in an image or transcribing words not spoken in audio.
failures.example: Agent receives an invoice image via Image(url='https://...') and returns extracted line items that include fabricated product names and prices not visible in the actual document.

failures.name: Fabricated Session Memory Recall
failures.description: Agno agents using SqliteStorage or PostgresStorage for session persistence may generate responses that reference past conversations that never occurred, especially when the agent is prompted to summarize prior interactions.
failures.example: An agent with storage=SqliteStorage(table_name='sessions', db_file='agent.db') claims the user previously approved a transaction that has no record in the session database.

failures.name: Nested Pydantic Schema Validation Failure
failures.description: Agno's response_model parameter accepts Pydantic BaseModel classes, but deeply nested schemas with List[BaseModel] fields or Optional unions often result in partially populated objects that pass isinstance checks but contain None where values are expected.
failures.example: A response_model with a nested List[LineItem] field returns an object where line_items is an empty list instead of raising a validation error, causing silent data loss in downstream processing.

failures.name: Team Agent Contradictory Synthesis
failures.description: In an Agno Team with multiple specialized agents, one sub-agent may return a recommendation that directly contradicts another's output. The team leader agent synthesizes these into a final response that is internally inconsistent.
failures.example: A financial analysis Team has a risk_agent recommending 'sell' and a sentiment_agent recommending 'buy' for the same asset. The team leader merges both into a response that advises both actions simultaneously.

failures.name: Tool Call Result Injection
failures.description: Agno agents with registered tools (via @agent.tool or the tools=[] parameter) may misuse tool outputs by treating error responses or empty results as valid data, propagating tool failures into the final agent response.
failures.example: An agent with a web search tool receives a 429 rate-limit error response and proceeds to fabricate search results rather than acknowledging the tool failure, returning invented citations.

steps.title: Install Dependencies
steps.description: Install both Agno and the Vex SDK into your Python environment. Vex requires Python 3.9+ and works with all Agno agent types including single agents, multi-modal agents, and Team configurations.
steps.code:
pip install agno vex-sdk

# Verify installation
python -c "import agno; import vex; print('agno:', agno.__version__, '| vex: OK')"

steps.title: Configure Vex and Wrap a Basic Agno Agent
steps.description: Initialize the Vex client with your API key and wrap your Agno agent's run() call using the vex.verify() decorator. This intercepts the agent response before it is returned to your application code.
steps.code:
import vex
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat

# Initialize Vex client
vex_client = vex.Client(api_key="your-vex-api-key")

# Define your Agno agent
agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    description="You are a financial analysis assistant.",
    markdown=True
)

# Wrap agent.run() with vex.verify()
@vex_client.verify(
    checks=["hallucination", "factual_consistency", "output_schema"],
    on_failure="raise"
)
def run_agent(user_message: str):
    return agent.run(user_message)

# Use exactly like agent.run()
response = run_agent("Summarize the Q3 earnings report.")
print(response.content)

steps.title: Validate Multi-Modal and Structured Output Agents
steps.description: For Agno agents using Image() inputs or Pydantic response_model schemas, configure Vex checks specific to multi-modal fidelity and schema completeness. This catches misinterpretations and silent partial-population failures.
steps.code:
import vex
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.media import Image
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List

vex_client = vex.Client(api_key="your-vex-api-key")

# Define a complex nested response schema
class LineItem(BaseModel):
    description: str
    quantity: int
    unit_price: float
    total: float

class InvoiceExtraction(BaseModel):
    vendor_name: str
    invoice_number: str
    line_items: List[LineItem]
    total_amount: float

# Multi-modal agent with structured output
agent = Agent(
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    response_model=InvoiceExtraction,
    description="Extract structured invoice data from images."
)

@vex_client.verify(
    checks=[
        "schema_completeness",      # Ensures no required fields are None
        "multimodal_grounding",     # Validates output is grounded in image content
        "numeric_consistency"       # Checks line_items totals sum to total_amount
    ],
    response_model=InvoiceExtraction,
    on_failure="raise"
)
def extract_invoice(image_url: str) -> InvoiceExtraction:
    return agent.run(
        "Extract all invoice data from this image.",
        images=[Image(url=image_url)]
    )

try:
    invoice = extract_invoice("https://example.com/invoice.png")
    print(f"Vendor: {invoice.vendor_name}, Total: {invoice.total_amount}")
except vex.VerificationError as e:
    print(f"Vex caught a failure: {e.check_name} - {e.message}")

steps.title: Guard Agno Team Agents Against Contradictory Synthesis
steps.description: For Agno Team configurations, apply Vex verification at both the sub-agent and team leader levels. Use the consistency check to detect when the synthesized team response contradicts individual sub-agent outputs.
steps.code:
import vex
from agno.agent import Agent
from agno.team import Team
from agno.models.openai import OpenAIChat
from agno.models.anthropic import Claude

vex_client = vex.Client(api_key="your-vex-api-key")

# Define specialized sub-agents
risk_agent = Agent(
    name="RiskAnalyst",
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    description="Analyze investment risk and provide buy/sell/hold recommendations.",
    role="Assess downside risk for the given asset."
)

sentiment_agent = Agent(
    name="SentimentAnalyst",
    model=Claude(id="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"),
    description="Analyze market sentiment from news and social signals.",
    role="Assess market sentiment and momentum."
)

# Create the Agno Team
analysis_team = Team(
    name="InvestmentAnalysisTeam",
    agents=[risk_agent, sentiment_agent],
    model=OpenAIChat(id="gpt-4o"),
    description="Synthesize risk and sentiment analysis into a unified recommendation.",
    mode="coordinate"
)

@vex_client.verify(
    checks=[
        "internal_consistency",   # Detects contradictory buy/sell signals in final output
        "sub_agent_alignment",    # Flags when synthesis ignores sub-agent conclusions
        "hallucination"           # Catches fabricated market data in the synthesis
    ],
    on_failure="warn",            # Log warnings instead of raising for team agents
    metadata={"team": "InvestmentAnalysisTeam", "mode": "coordinate"}
)
def run_investment_analysis(ticker: str, context: str) -> str:
    return analysis_team.run(
        f"Provide a unified investment recommendation for {ticker}. Context: {context}"
    )

result = run_investment_analysis(
    ticker="NVDA",
    context="Q4 earnings beat by 15%, but macro headwinds increasing."
)
print(result.content)

tips.title: Apply Vex Verification at the Agent Boundary, Not Inside Tools
tips.description: Wrap agent.run() at the outermost call site rather than inside individual Agno tool functions. This ensures Vex evaluates the complete agent response including all tool call results that have been incorporated, giving it full context to detect fabricated tool outputs or inconsistent synthesis.

tips.title: Use response_model with Vex schema_completeness for All Structured Outputs
tips.description: Always pair Agno's response_model=YourPydanticModel parameter with Vex's schema_completeness check. Agno will not raise an error when optional nested fields are silently omitted, so Vex must enforce that all semantically required fields are populated before the response reaches downstream code.

tips.title: Tag Vex Verifications with Agno Agent Names for Observability
tips.description: Pass the Agno agent's name and model ID into Vex's metadata parameter so that failures are attributed correctly in the Vex dashboard. Use metadata={'agent_name': agent.name, 'model': agent.model.id, 'session_id': agent.session_id} to correlate Vex alerts with specific Agno session logs.

tips.title: Set on_failure='warn' During Development, 'raise' in Production
tips.description: During development and prompt iteration, configure Vex with on_failure='warn' so verification failures are logged without interrupting your testing loop. Switch to on_failure='raise' in production so that vex.VerificationError exceptions are caught by your error handling middleware and the unverified response is never returned to end users.

tips.title: Validate Session Memory Reads Separately from Agent Responses
tips.description: When using Agno's SqliteStorage or PostgresStorage for session persistence, add a dedicated Vex memory_consistency check that compares claims the agent makes about past interactions against the actual stored session records. This requires passing the session storage contents as ground_truth context to vex.verify() so it can detect fabricated recall.

cta.heading: Start Securing Your Agno Agents
cta.description: Add Vex to your Agno project today and get runtime guardrails for multi-modal inputs, structured outputs, session memory, and team coordination in under 10 minutes.
